1-12.667.025Z"></path></svg></button></div></div></div><div class="m-auto max-w-[768px] p-4"><div itemprop="articleBody"><h3 class="mb-6">Key Takeaways</h3><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">The USD is likely to stay in a relatively tight range as markets debate the timing of Fed cuts, while geopolitical risk keeps a “safe haven” layer in play.</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Higher oil prices are supporting energy exporters, with WTI near <b class="font-semibold">US$74.00</b> and Canada positioned to benefit at the margin.</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Policy divergence remains the core 2026 theme, with the RBA already hiking while Europe holds steady and the BoE faces renewed cut pricing.</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base">As we move deeper into 2026, global currency markets are navigating a steady growth backdrop, but with more event risk and policy divergence than the “one-way USD” environment that defined long stretches of the last cycle. March begins with FX pricing pulled between rate expectations, geopolitics, and commodity dynamics.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">According to the IMF’s January 2026 World Economic Outlook update, global GDP growth is projected at <b class="font-semibold">3.3%</b> in <b class="font-semibold">2026²</b>. A steadier growth environment can support risk-sensitive currencies at the margin, but it also increases the importance of relative performance. In practice, FX moves can be driven by “who looks stronger” and “who is cutting first,” rather than a single dominant theme.¹ ²</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="global-themes-shaping-currency-markets-in-2026" class="mb-6">Global Themes Shaping Currency Markets In 2026</h2><p class="mb-6 text-base">Several macro forces are defining currency dynamics as the year continues:</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Policy divergence is back:</b> Markets are pricing further Fed cuts by year-end while expecting no near-term change from the ECB, and Australia has already restarted its hiking cycle.¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">The USD remains resilient, but headline-sensitive:</b> Uncertainty around near-term Fed cuts is keeping the USD range-bound, while geopolitical developments can add a safe-haven bid.¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Geopolitics is influencing risk appetite:</b> Military strikes in Iran have increased demand for liquid safe havens, supporting the USD and, potentially, CHF.¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Commodities, especially oil, matter again:</b> WTI has lifted to around <b class="font-semibold">US$74.00¹</b>, supporting energy exporters like Canada relative to energy importers.¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Trade and tariffs remain a volatility source:</b> Tariff developments are adding uncertainty, even when the USD does not react in a straightforward way.¹</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="us-dollar-range-trading-with-a-safe-haven-layer" class="mb-6">U.S. Dollar: Range Trading, With A Safe-Haven Layer</h2><p class="mb-6 text-base">The report frames the USD as largely range-bound because markets remain uncertain about the timing of Fed rate cuts.¹ US growth slowed to <b class="font-semibold">1.4%</b> (SAAR) in the first estimate, down from <b class="font-semibold">4.4%</b> (SAAR) in Q3, with the government shutdown weighing on government spending and local investment.¹ Inflation signals are mixed: PCE rose to <b class="font-semibold">2.9%¹</b>, while CPI eased to <b class="font-semibold">2.4%</b> in January and core CPI held at <b class="font-semibold">2.5%¹</b>. The labour market looks stable, with nonfarm payrolls up <b class="font-semibold">130K¹</b> in January and unemployment at <b class="font-semibold">4.3%¹</b> (with annual revisions showing job gains averaged <b class="font-semibold">34K</b> fewer per month than initially reported¹).</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">Policy is also “steady for now.” The Fed held rates at <b class="font-semibold">3.50% to 3.75%</b> in January and is framed as patient ahead of its next meeting on March 18.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">The March twist is geopolitics. The report notes that military strikes in Iran may maintain a safe-haven bid for the USD because of the dollar’s vast liquidity.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">What this means for businesses</b><br> When the USD is range-bound but headline-sensitive, the practical risk is getting forced into one execution window. A common approach is:</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Stage larger conversions rather than relying on a single timestamp</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Align coverage to due dates for firm payables</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Pull approvals forward so execution is a choice, not a deadline</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="europe-and-the-uk-resilience-in-europe-rate-cut-risk-in-the-uk" class="mb-6">Europe And The UK: Resilience In Europe, Rate-Cut Risk In The UK</h2><h3 class="mb-6">Euro (EUR)</h3><p class="mb-6 text-base">The Eurozone economy is described as resilient, with unemployment at <b class="font-semibold">6.2%¹</b> and inflation easing to <b class="font-semibold">1.7%</b> in January from <b class="font-semibold">1.9%</b> in December.¹ The ECB kept rates unchanged at <b class="font-semibold">2.0%</b> in early February and meets again on March 19, with markets not pricing near-term changes to official rates.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">Against the USD, the report suggests EUR/USD is consolidating after moving above <b class="font-semibold">1.2000¹</b> earlier in the year, with the Eurozone-US two-year differential supportive of a <b class="font-semibold">1.1650 to 1.1850¹</b> range.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Business takeaway for EUR payers</b><br> A “range” can still mean meaningful swings for operating budgets. If EUR invoices are committed:</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Cover known EUR payables earlier in the cycle</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Keep forecast amounts flexible</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Avoid last-day processing that can add operational risk to market risk</p></li></ul><h3 class="mb-6">British Pound (GBP)</h3><p class="mb-6 text-base">The UK picture is more mixed. GDP rose <b class="font-semibold">0.1%</b> in Q4, leaving growth at <b class="font-semibold">0.7%</b> year over year.¹ Retail sales lifted to <b class="font-semibold">4.5%</b> year over year in January, a six-year high.¹ But unemployment has risen to <b class="font-semibold">5.2%¹</b>, while inflation fell to <b class="font-semibold">3.0%</b> in January from <b class="font-semibold">3.4%</b> in December (with services inflation still high at <b class="font-semibold">4.4%¹</b>).¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">The BoE held rates at <b class="font-semibold">3.75%</b> on February 5, but the vote split was dovish, with <b class="font-semibold">4 of 9</b> members voting to cut.¹ Markets are pricing a <b class="font-semibold">72%</b> chance of a BoE cut on March 19, which the report flags as a potential downside driver for GBP/USD.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Business takeaway for GBP payers</b><br> If your costs are GBP-linked, the operational win is reducing the quote-to-pay gap:</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Tighten quote validity windows with suppliers</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Keep approvals predictable ahead of central bank weeks</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="asia-pacific-aud-stronger-nzd-patient" class="mb-6">Asia-Pacific: AUD Stronger, NZD Patient</h2><p class="mb-6 text-base">Australia is the standout in the report. The RBA lifted rates <b class="font-semibold">25bp</b> to <b class="font-semibold">3.85%</b> on February 3, the first hike of this cycle, noting growth and inflation were stronger than expected.¹ Australia’s unemployment rate held at <b class="font-semibold">4.1%¹</b>, while inflation remained elevated at <b class="font-semibold">3.8%</b> in January and trimmed mean inflation rose to <b class="font-semibold">3.4%</b>, a 16-month high.¹ Markets assign a <b class="font-semibold">16%</b> chance of another hike at the March 17 RBA meeting.¹ With the USD expected to remain range-bound, the report suggests AUD/USD may have room to push above <b class="font-semibold">0.7200¹</b> over coming months.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">New Zealand’s economy continues to improve, but the RBNZ has signaled patience. The RBNZ held rates at <b class="font-semibold">2.25%</b> and indicated rates will remain steady “for some time.”¹ NZ employment growth is up <b class="font-semibold">0.2%</b> year over year, but unemployment has lifted to <b class="font-semibold">5.4%¹</b>, the highest since September 2015.¹ The report frames NZD/USD as easing in February and potentially staying within a <b class="font-semibold">0.5925 to 0.6120¹</b> range near term.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Business takeaway for APAC payers</b><br> AUD and NZD can move quickly around policy and data windows, often outside North American business hours. If you have APAC payables:</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Confirm approval cutoffs across time zones</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Avoid leaving execution to the final day</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Consider staging larger conversions for predictability</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="japan-and-china-policy-constraints-and-structural-support" class="mb-6">Japan And China: Policy Constraints And Structural Support</h2><h3 class="mb-6">Japanese Yen (JPY)</h3><p class="mb-6 text-base">Japan’s economy is growing modestly, up <b class="font-semibold">0.1%</b> quarter over quarter and <b class="font-semibold">0.1%</b> year over year in the December quarter.¹ Services activity is firmer, with the services PMI at <b class="font-semibold">53.7¹</b> in January, while inflation eased to <b class="font-semibold">1.5%</b> in January from <b class="font-semibold">2.1%</b> in December.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">The BoJ policy rate is <b class="font-semibold">0.75%</b>, and while the BoJ still signals gradual rate increases over time, the report suggests political developments and falling inflation make a near-term move less likely.¹ The report also highlights fiscal concerns, including plans to suspend the <b class="font-semibold">8%</b> consumption tax on food and beverages for two years, worth about <b class="font-semibold">¥5 trillion</b> annually and <b class="font-semibold">7.2%</b> of total tax revenue.¹ The net effect in the report is continued JPY sensitivity to policy expectations and fiscal debate, with USD/JPY expected to remain firm and potentially push toward <b class="font-semibold">158.00¹</b>.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Business takeaway for JPY payers</b><br> JPY risk is often about volatility, not direction. If you pay Japan-based suppliers, reducing last-day execution risk is usually the most practical win.</p><h3 class="mb-6">Chinese Yuan (CNY)</h3><p class="mb-6 text-base">CNY strength continued through February in the report’s framing.¹ Domestic demand remains challenged, but exports are described as surging, and authorities appear to favor a “strong yuan policy,” with the trade surplus above <b class="font-semibold">4.0%</b> of GDP.¹ The report notes that nearly half of China’s cross-border transactions are now denominated in CNY, supporting demand.¹ USD/CNY is projected at <b class="font-semibold">6.8582¹</b> with a downside bias in the report, though it also notes the possibility of PBoC cuts ahead of the National People’s Congress on March 5, which could create temporary upward pressure in USD/CNY.¹</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><b class="font-semibold">Business takeaway for CNY payers</b><br> Operational accuracy matters. In periods of heightened bank checks, clean beneficiary data and consistent payment references can reduce delays more than trying to time a better level.</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="key-risks-to-watch-in-2026" class="mb-6">Key Risks To Watch In 2026</h2><p class="mb-6 text-base">While the baseline outlook is constructive, the report highlights risks that could drive volatility:¹</p><ul class="my-8 ml-8 list-disc pl-5 "><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">USD moves driven by Fed cut timing and geopolitical developments</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">EUR/USD spending more time above <b class="font-semibold">1.2000¹</b> later in 2026 if USD softens and ECB holds steady¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">GBP sensitivity to whether the BoE begins cutting earlier than expected¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">AUD upside if the RBA delivers a series of rate increases, with AUD/USD projected toward <b class="font-semibold">0.7500¹</b> over 2026¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">NZD outperformance later in 2026 if the RBNZ begins hiking¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">USD/JPY volatility as higher Japanese rates raise debt-servicing concerns¹</p></li><li class="[&_p]:mb-0"><p class="mb-6 text-base">Volatility spikes in CAD and MXN as USMCA negotiations intensify ahead of July 2026¹</p></li></ul><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br></p><h2 id="bottom-line" class="mb-6">Bottom Line</h2><p class="mb-6 text-base">March 2026 reinforces the core 2026 theme: policy divergence and relative performance are driving currency markets again.¹ With the USD likely to remain range-bound but headline-sensitive, and commodities and geopolitics back in focus, businesses are best served by staying proactive. Cover what you already know you must pay, reduce last-day payment risk, and keep execution predictable.</p><p class="mb-6 text-base">At Xe, we will continue monitoring these developments closely, helping you navigate currency movements with clarity and confidence throughout 2026.</p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br> </p><div><a class="polymorphic-btn items-center justify-center gap-2 font-semibold transition-colors duration-200 focus:outline focus:outline-2 focus:outline-offset-2 fit-content bg-blue text-white hover:bg-blue-450 focus:outline-blue-500 px-6 py-3 rounded-lg text-[0.95rem] flex flex-row-reverse [&_svg]:hover:translate-x-1 [&_svg]:transition-transform [&_svg]:duration-200 m-auto w-fit" title=" Download the Global Currency Outlook (PDF), " href="https://resources.xe.com/xe-global-currency-outlook-march-2026"><span class="relative top-[1px]"> Download the Global Currency Outlook (PDF) </span></a></div><p class="mb-6 text-base"><br><br> ‎ </p><hr class="border-gray-300"><p class="mb-6 text-base"></p><p class="mb-6 text-base"><em>The content within this blog post is not intended for use as financial advice. gold_text:
Key Takeaways
The USD is likely to stay in a relatively tight range as markets debate the timing of Fed cuts, while geopolitical risk keeps a “safe haven” layer in play.
Higher oil prices are supporting energy exporters, with WTI near US$74.00 and Canada positioned to benefit at the margin.
Policy divergence remains the core 2026 theme, with the RBA already hiking while Europe holds steady and the BoE faces renewed cut pricing.
As we move deeper into 2026, global currency markets are navigating a steady growth backdrop, but with more event risk and policy divergence than the “one-way USD” environment that defined long stretches of the last cycle. March begins with FX pricing pulled between rate expectations, geopolitics, and commodity dynamics.¹
According to the IMF’s January 2026 World Economic Outlook update, global GDP growth is projected at 3.3% in 2026². A steadier growth environment can support risk-sensitive currencies at the margin, but it also increases the importance of relative performance. In practice, FX moves can be driven by “who looks stronger” and “who is cutting first,” rather than a single dominant theme.¹ ²
Global Themes Shaping Currency Markets In 2026
Several macro forces are defining currency dynamics as the year continues:
Policy divergence is back: Markets are pricing further Fed cuts by year-end while expecting no near-term change from the ECB, and Australia has already restarted its hiking cycle.¹
The USD remains resilient, but headline-sensitive: Uncertainty around near-term Fed cuts is keeping the USD range-bound, while geopolitical developments can add a safe-haven bid.¹
Geopolitics is influencing risk appetite: Military strikes in Iran have increased demand for liquid safe havens, supporting the USD and, potentially, CHF.¹
Commodities, especially oil, matter again: WTI has lifted to around US$74.00¹, supporting energy exporters like Canada relative to energy importers.¹
Trade and tariffs remain a volatility source: Tariff developments are adding uncertainty, even when the USD does not react in a straightforward way.¹
U.S. Dollar: Range Trading, With A Safe-Haven Layer
The report frames the USD as largely range-bound because markets remain uncertain about the timing of Fed rate cuts.¹ US growth slowed to 1.4% (SAAR) in the first estimate, down from 4.4% (SAAR) in Q3, with the government shutdown weighing on government spending and local investment.¹ Inflation signals are mixed: PCE rose to 2.9%¹, while CPI eased to 2.4% in January and core CPI held at 2.5%¹. The labour market looks stable, with nonfarm payrolls up 130K¹ in January and unemployment at 4.3%¹ (with annual revisions showing job gains averaged 34K fewer per month than initially reported¹).
Policy is also “steady for now.” The Fed held rates at 3.50% to 3.75% in January and is framed as patient ahead of its next meeting on March 18.¹
The March twist is geopolitics. The report notes that military strikes in Iran may maintain a safe-haven bid for the USD because of the dollar’s vast liquidity.¹
What this means for businesses
When the USD is range-bound but headline-sensitive, the practical risk is getting forced into one execution window. A common approach is:
Stage larger conversions rather than relying on a single timestamp
Align coverage to due dates for firm payables
Pull approvals forward so execution is a choice, not a deadline
Europe And The UK: Resilience In Europe, Rate-Cut Risk In The UK
Euro (EUR)
The Eurozone economy is described as resilient, with unemployment at 6.2%¹ and inflation easing to 1.7% in January from 1.9% in December.¹ The ECB kept rates unchanged at 2.0% in early February and meets again on March 19, with markets not pricing near-term changes to official rates.¹
Against the USD, the report suggests EUR/USD is consolidating after moving above 1.2000¹ earlier in the year, with the Eurozone-US two-year differential supportive of a 1.1650 to 1.1850¹ range.¹
Business takeaway for EUR payers
A “range” can still mean meaningful swings for operating budgets. If EUR invoices are committed:
Cover known EUR payables earlier in the cycle
Keep forecast amounts flexible
Avoid last-day processing that can add operational risk to market risk
British Pound (GBP)
The UK picture is more mixed. GDP rose 0.1% in Q4, leaving growth at 0.7% year over year.¹ Retail sales lifted to 4.5% year over year in January, a six-year high.¹ But unemployment has risen to 5.2%¹, while inflation fell to 3.0% in January from 3.4% in December (with services inflation still high at 4.4%¹).¹
The BoE held rates at 3.75% on February 5, but the vote split was dovish, with 4 of 9 members voting to cut.¹ Markets are pricing a 72% chance of a BoE cut on March 19, which the report flags as a potential downside driver for GBP/USD.¹
Business takeaway for GBP payers
If your costs are GBP-linked, the operational win is reducing the quote-to-pay gap:
Tighten quote validity windows with suppliers
Keep approvals predictable ahead of central bank weeks
Keep approvals predictable ahead of central bank weeks
Australia is the standout in the report. The RBA lifted rates 25bp to 3.85% on February 3, the first hike of this cycle, noting growth and inflation were stronger than expected.¹ Australia’s unemployment rate held at 4.1%¹, while inflation remained elevated at 3.8% in January and trimmed mean inflation rose to 3.4%, a 16-month high.¹ Markets assign a 16% chance of another hike at the March 17 RBA meeting.¹ With the USD expected to remain range-bound, the report suggests AUD/USD may have room to push above 0.7200¹ over coming months.¹
New Zealand’s economy continues to improve, but the RBNZ has signaled patience. The RBNZ held rates at 2.25% and indicated rates will remain steady “for some time.”¹ NZ employment growth is up 0.2% year over year, but unemployment has lifted to 5.4%¹, the highest since September 2015.¹ The report frames NZD/USD as easing in February and potentially staying within a 0.5925 to 0.6120¹ range near term.¹
Business takeaway for APAC payers
AUD and NZD can move quickly around policy and data windows, often outside North American business hours. If you have APAC payables:
Confirm approval cutoffs across time zones
Avoid leaving execution to the final day
Consider staging larger conversions for predictability
Japan And China: Policy Constraints And Structural Support
Japanese Yen (JPY)
Japan’s economy is growing modestly, up 0.1% quarter over quarter and 0.1% year over year in the December quarter.¹ Services activity is firmer, with the services PMI at 53.7¹ in January, while inflation eased to 1.5% in January from 2.1% in December.¹
The BoJ policy rate is 0.75%, and while the BoJ still signals gradual rate increases over time, the report suggests political developments and falling inflation make a near-term move less likely.¹ The report also highlights fiscal concerns, including plans to suspend the 8% consumption tax on food and beverages for two years, worth about ¥5 trillion annually and 7.2% of total tax revenue.¹ The net effect in the report is continued JPY sensitivity to policy expectations and fiscal debate, with USD/JPY expected to remain firm and potentially push toward 158.00¹.¹
Business takeaway for JPY payers
JPY risk is often about volatility, not direction. If you pay Japan-based suppliers, reducing last-day execution risk is usually the most practical win.
Chinese Yuan (CNY)
CNY strength continued through February in the report’s framing.¹ Domestic demand remains challenged, but exports are described as surging, and authorities appear to favor a “strong yuan policy,” with the trade surplus above 4.0% of GDP.¹ The report notes that nearly half of China’s cross-border transactions are now denominated in CNY, supporting demand.¹ USD/CNY is projected at 6.8582¹ with a downside bias in the report, though it also notes the possibility of PBoC cuts ahead of the National People’s Congress on March 5, which could create temporary upward pressure in USD/CNY.¹
Business takeaway for CNY payers
Operational accuracy matters. In periods of heightened bank checks, clean beneficiary data and consistent payment references can reduce delays more than trying to time a better level.
Key Risks To Watch In 2026

Bottom Line
